_comment: Curated sports facts. by_date keys are MM-DD for events that happened ON that date; general is a rotating pool. Every entry is tagged with a sport for the stats view. PENDING USER REVIEW — verify before relying on any single fact. Skip-listed facts (Don Larsen perfect game, Miracle on Ice, Wilt's 100, called shot) are intentionally absent.

01-01.text: On January 1, 1902, the first Rose Bowl was played in Pasadena — Michigan crushed Stanford 49-0 so badly that the game wasn't played again for 14 years.

01-15.text: On January 15, 1967, the first Super Bowl was played at the LA Coliseum. The Packers beat the Chiefs 35-10, and the game wasn't even a sellout — tickets cost as little as $6.

04-08.text: On April 8, 1974, Hank Aaron hit home run number 715 off Al Downing in Atlanta, breaking Babe Ruth's record while carrying years of hate mail with grace.

04-15.text: On April 15, 1947, Jackie Robinson debuted for the Brooklyn Dodgers at Ebbets Field, breaking baseball's color line. Every MLB player now wears his number 42 each April 15.

04-23.text: On April 23, 1954, Hank Aaron hit the first of his 755 career home runs — off Cardinals pitcher Vic Raschi at Sportsman's Park in St. Louis.

05-02.text: On May 2, 1939, Lou Gehrig took himself out of the lineup after 2,130 consecutive games, ending the streak quietly: he told his manager, 'I'm benching myself, for the good of the team.'

05-06.text: On May 6, 1954, Roger Bannister ran the first sub-4-minute mile — 3:59.4 on a windy day at Oxford, in a lunch-break race, after rounds at the hospital where he worked.

05-13.text: On May 13, 1958, Stan Musial doubled at Wrigley Field for career hit number 3,000 — he'd been held out of the starting lineup so the milestone could happen in St. Louis, then delivered it as a pinch hitter anyway.

05-15.text: On May 15, 1941, Joe DiMaggio singled against the White Sox — the quiet start of his 56-game hitting streak, a record that has stood for more than 80 years.

05-17.text: On May 17, 1875, the first Kentucky Derby was run at Churchill Downs, won by a colt named Aristides in front of about 10,000 spectators.

05-18.text: On May 18, 2004, Randy Johnson became the oldest pitcher to throw a perfect game at age 40, retiring all 27 Atlanta Braves batters he faced.

05-25.text: On May 25, 1935, Jesse Owens had the greatest 45 minutes in sports history at the Big Ten championships: he set three world records and tied a fourth — with a sore back, in four different events.

05-26.text: On May 26, 1959, Pirates pitcher Harvey Haddix threw 12 perfect innings against the Braves — 36 up, 36 down — and still lost the game in the 13th.

05-28.text: On May 28, 1957, National League owners voted to let the Dodgers and Giants leave New York for Los Angeles and San Francisco — and big-league baseball finally reached the West Coast.

06-09.text: On June 9, 1973, Secretariat won the Belmont Stakes by 31 lengths to claim the Triple Crown. His time of 2:24 for a mile and a half has never been touched.

06-10.text: On June 10, 1944, 15-year-old Joe Nuxhall pitched for the Cincinnati Reds — still the youngest player in MLB history. He didn't appear in another big-league game for eight years.

06-11.text: On June 11, 1997, Michael Jordan played the 'Flu Game' — visibly ill and barely able to stand, he scored 38 points to beat the Jazz in Game 5 of the Finals.

06-12.text: On June 12, 2019, the Blues won the Stanley Cup — Game 7 in Boston, 4-1 — their first championship in 52 seasons of trying, after sitting dead last in the league that January.

06-14.text: On June 14, 1998, Michael Jordan hit 'The Last Shot' over Bryon Russell to win his sixth title — 45 points in Game 6, the final shot of his Bulls career.

06-15.text: On June 15, 1938, Johnny Vander Meer threw his second consecutive no-hitter — the only back-to-back no-nos in MLB history — in the first night game ever played at Ebbets Field.

06-17.text: On June 17, 1994, the World Cup kicked off on US soil for the first time, at Soldier Field in Chicago. The tournament still holds the World Cup attendance record.

06-19.text: On June 19, 1846, the first organized baseball game under modern rules was played at Elysian Fields in Hoboken, New Jersey. The Knickerbockers — who wrote the rules — lost 23-1.

06-23.text: On June 23, 1972, Title IX was signed into law. Women's sports scholarships were nearly nonexistent then; the law transformed American athletics within a generation.

07-04.text: On July 4, 1939, Lou Gehrig told a packed Yankee Stadium, 'Today I consider myself the luckiest man on the face of the earth' — two weeks after his ALS diagnosis.

07-16.text: On July 16, 1950, Uruguay silenced roughly 200,000 fans at the Maracanã by beating Brazil 2-1 in the World Cup's deciding match — the 'Maracanazo,' still soccer's greatest upset.

08-03.text: On August 3, 1936, Jesse Owens won the 100 meters at the Berlin Olympics — the first of four gold medals he took in front of Hitler.

09-06.text: On September 6, 1995, Cal Ripken Jr. played his 2,131st consecutive game, passing Lou Gehrig. The ovation lasted 22 minutes and he was pushed into a lap around Camden Yards.

09-08.text: On September 8, 1998, Mark McGwire hit home run number 62 off Steve Trachsel at Busch Stadium, breaking Roger Maris's record — and hugged the Maris family in the front row.

09-20.text: On September 20, 1973, Billie Jean King beat Bobby Riggs in the 'Battle of the Sexes' before 30,000 at the Astrodome and a TV audience of 90 million.

10-01.text: On October 1, 1961, Roger Maris hit home run number 61 off Tracy Stallard on the season's final day, breaking Babe Ruth's single-season record under crushing pressure.

10-18.text: On October 18, 1977, Reggie Jackson hit three home runs on three consecutive pitches — off three different pitchers — to clinch the World Series. 'Mr. October' was born.

10-20.text: On October 20, 1968, Dick Fosbury won Olympic high jump gold going over the bar backwards. The 'Fosbury Flop' was mocked at the time; today every elite jumper uses it.

10-27.text: On October 27, 2011, David Freese — a St. Louis kid — saved the Cardinals with a two-strike, two-out triple in the 9th, then won Game 6 with a walk-off homer in the 11th. 'We will see you tomorrow night!'

10-30.text: On October 30, 1974, Muhammad Ali knocked out George Foreman in the 'Rumble in the Jungle' in Zaire, using the rope-a-dope to exhaust the most feared puncher alive.

11-25.text: On November 25, 1980, Roberto Durán turned away from Sugar Ray Leonard in the eighth round muttering 'No más' — boxing's most famous surrender.

12-23.text: On December 23, 1972, Franco Harris caught a deflected pass off his shoetops and ran it in — the 'Immaculate Reception,' still the most debated play in NFL history.

12-28.text: On December 28, 1958, the Colts beat the Giants 23-17 in the NFL's first sudden-death overtime — 'The Greatest Game Ever Played,' the broadcast that made pro football America's TV sport.

general.text: Stan Musial finished with exactly 3,630 hits: 1,815 at home and 1,815 on the road. Perfect symmetry over 22 seasons.
Bob Gibson's 1.12 ERA in 1968 was so dominant that MLB lowered the mound and shrank the strike zone the following season.
In 1999, Cardinal Fernando Tatis hit two grand slams in the same inning — both off the same pitcher, Chan Ho Park. No one else has ever done it.
The 1944 World Series was an all-St. Louis affair — Cardinals vs. Browns — with every game played at Sportsman's Park. The teams literally shared the stadium.
In 1951, Browns owner Bill Veeck sent 3-foot-7 Eddie Gaedel to pinch-hit wearing number 1/8. He walked on four pitches and was banned from baseball the next day.
Rogers Hornsby hit .424 for the Cardinals in 1924 — the highest batting average of the modern era, and nobody has come within 30 points of it since.
Ozzie Smith won 13 consecutive Gold Gloves at shortstop and did a backflip on Opening Day. The Wizard earned the nickname.
Cy Young won 511 games. The next-closest pitcher, Walter Johnson, is 94 wins behind — a gap bigger than most pitchers' entire careers.
Nolan Ryan threw seven no-hitters, the last one at age 44 — an age when most pitchers have been retired for a decade.
Rickey Henderson stole 1,406 bases. Second place is 468 steals behind. He also scored more runs than anyone who ever played.
Ted Williams hit .406 in 1941 and refused to sit out the final-day doubleheader to protect the mark — he went 6-for-8 instead.
Cal Ripken Jr. played 2,632 consecutive games over 16 years — a record many believe will never be broken.
The 1906 Cubs won 116 games and lost the World Series to the crosstown White Sox — a team nicknamed the 'Hitless Wonders' for their league-worst batting average.
Ichiro Suzuki collected 262 hits in 2004, breaking an 84-year-old single-season record — and he didn't reach MLB until he was 27.
Connie Mack managed the Philadelphia Athletics for 50 consecutive seasons, wearing a suit and tie in the dugout the entire time.
In 1982, Joel Youngblood got hits for two different teams in two different cities on the same day — traded mid-day from the Mets, he flew to Philadelphia and singled for the Expos that night.
Satchel Paige became MLB's oldest rookie at 42 after two decades of Negro Leagues stardom — and still helped pitch Cleveland to the 1948 pennant.
The 1869 Cincinnati Red Stockings were baseball's first openly professional team. They went undefeated on a national tour, winning around 60 straight games.
Wrigley Field, which opened in 1914, didn't host a night game until August 1988 — the last big-league park to install lights.
Yogi Berra won 10 World Series rings as a player — more than any other player in history, and more than 24 of the 30 current franchises.
Mariano Rivera is the only player ever elected to the Hall of Fame unanimously — and he did it throwing essentially one pitch for 19 years.
In 2024, Shohei Ohtani became the first player in MLB history with 50 home runs and 50 stolen bases in a season — he finished with 54 and 59.
Walter Johnson threw 110 shutouts in his career. No active pitcher has 15.
Michael Jordan hit .202 in Double-A Birmingham during his 1994 baseball detour — and baseball people respected that it wasn't worse, given he hadn't played since high school.
Wilt Chamberlain averaged 50.4 points per game for the entire 1961-62 NBA season — and played all but 8 minutes of the year.
The 1980 NBA Finals MVP went to Magic Johnson — a rookie who started at center in Game 6 because Kareem was injured. He scored 42 points and grabbed 15 rebounds.
Bill Russell won 11 championships in 13 seasons. No player in any major American sport has more rings than Russell has fingers.
UCLA won 10 national titles in 12 years under John Wooden, including an 88-game winning streak that lasted nearly three full seasons.
The NBA's 24-second shot clock was invented in 1954 by Syracuse owner Danny Biasone, who did the math on a napkin: 48 minutes ÷ 120 expected shots = 24 seconds. It saved the league.
The St. Louis Hawks won the 1958 NBA championship behind Bob Pettit's 50 points in the clinching game — still the city's only NBA title.
Basketball at the 1936 Olympics was played outdoors on dirt courts. The gold-medal game was played in driving rain, and the US beat Canada 19-8.
Muggsy Bogues played 14 NBA seasons at 5-foot-3 — and blocked 39 shots, including one on Patrick Ewing.
Klay Thompson once scored 37 points in a single quarter — a perfect 13-for-13 from the field. The other team scored 24 that quarter.
Scott Skiles dished 30 assists in a single NBA game in 1990 — a record no one has come within 5 of since.
When the ABA folded into the NBA in 1976, it brought the 3-point line, the dunk contest, and four franchises: the Spurs, Nuggets, Pacers, and Nets.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's career scoring record of 38,387 points stood for nearly 39 years before LeBron James passed it in 2023.
The Stanley Cup is the oldest trophy awarded to a professional sports franchise in North America, dating back to 1893.
Wayne Gretzky has more career assists (1,963) than any other player has total points. You could erase every goal he ever scored and he'd still be the NHL's all-time scoring leader.
The Blues reached the Stanley Cup Final in each of their first three seasons (1968-70) — and were swept all three times. It took 49 more years to win it.
Brett Hull scored 86 goals for the Blues in 1990-91 — the most ever by anyone not named Gretzky.
Goalie Glenn Hall played 502 consecutive complete games — without a mask. He later backstopped the expansion Blues to three straight Cup Finals.
Bobby Orr is the only defenseman ever to lead the NHL in scoring — and he did it twice.
The 1919 Stanley Cup Final was cancelled mid-series due to the influenza pandemic — one of only two years the Cup has not been awarded.
The 'Original Six' era — just six NHL teams — lasted 25 years, until 1967, when the league doubled overnight and the expansion St. Louis Blues were born.
Teemu Selanne scored 76 goals as a rookie in 1992-93 — 23 more than any first-year player before or since.
Frank Zamboni invented his ice-resurfacing machine in 1949 for his Southern California rink. The family company still makes them, and his name became the word.
Tom Dempsey, born without toes on his kicking foot, hit a 63-yard field goal in 1970 — a record that stood for 43 years.
The largest blowout in NFL history is the 1940 championship: Bears 73, Washington 0. Washington had beaten the Bears three weeks earlier.
Jerry Rice's 22,895 receiving yards are almost 5,500 more than second place — roughly three full seasons of separation.
The 1972 Dolphins remain the NFL's only perfect season at 17-0 — and their starting QB missed most of the year with a broken leg.
The first pick of the first NFL draft in 1936, Heisman winner Jay Berwanger, never played a pro down — he asked for $1,000 a game and no one would pay it.
Kurt Warner was stocking grocery shelves in Iowa five years before leading the Rams to a Super Bowl title and winning two MVPs.
Marshall Faulk piled up 2,429 yards from scrimmage for the 1999 Rams — a record at the time — as the engine of the Greatest Show on Turf.
Deion Sanders is the only person to play in both a Super Bowl and a World Series. He also once played in an NFL and MLB game in the same week.
Walter Payton missed one game in 13 NFL seasons — as a rookie — and was furious about it for years.
In 1916, Georgia Tech beat Cumberland College 222-0. Cumberland had dropped its football program; a contract clause forced them to show up anyway.
Pelé is still the only player to win three World Cups — and he won his first at 17, scoring twice in the final.
When England lost to the US 1-0 at the 1950 World Cup — maybe the biggest upset in the sport's history — five of the American starters were from St. Louis.
The original World Cup trophy was stolen in London in 1966 and found a week later by a dog named Pickles. Stolen again in Brazil in 1983, it was never recovered.
The fastest World Cup goal ever came 10.8 seconds into a 2002 match, by Turkey's Hakan Şükür — before some TV viewers had found their seats.
Marta has scored 17 World Cup goals — more than any player in history, men's or women's tournament.
The US women have won four World Cups — no other country, men's or women's side combined, has more than Brazil's five.
In 2023, St. Louis City SC became the first MLS expansion team to win its conference's regular season in its debut year — and sold out every home match.
The 2026 World Cup is the first with 48 teams and three host countries — and the final will be played in New Jersey.
The longest tennis match ever lasted 11 hours and 5 minutes over three days: Isner beat Mahut 70-68 in the fifth set at Wimbledon 2010. The scoreboard wasn't programmed to go that high.
Serena Williams won the 2017 Australian Open — her 23rd major — while eight weeks pregnant, without dropping a set.
Steffi Graf's 1988 'Golden Slam' — all four majors plus Olympic gold in one year — has never been matched by any player.
Billie Jean King founded the WTA in 1973 and pushed the US Open to become the first major with equal prize money — that same year.
Roger Federer started as a ballboy at the Basel tournament he later won ten times.
Tiger Woods held all four major titles at once in 2000-01 — the 'Tiger Slam.' No golfer had done it since the modern Grand Slam existed.
Jack Nicklaus won 18 majors, including a sixth Masters at age 46 — shooting 30 on the back nine on Sunday.
In 1913, 20-year-old amateur Francis Ouimet won the US Open over the two best pros alive, with a 10-year-old caddie named Eddie Lowery carrying his bag.
Annika Sörenstam shot 59 in 2001 — still the only sub-60 round in LPGA history.
The Masters green jacket must stay at Augusta National — champions get to keep it at home for exactly one year, then return it to the club.
Eddie Eagan is the only athlete to win gold at both the Summer and Winter Olympics in different sports: boxing in 1920, bobsled in 1932.
Abebe Bikila won the 1960 Olympic marathon in Rome barefoot — then defended his title four years later in shoes, setting a world record both times.

The 1904 Olympics were held in St. Louis — the first Games on US soil. The marathon was chaos: the first finisher rode part of the route in a car, and the actual winner was dosed with strychnine and brandy.
Usain Bolt's 9.58-second 100 meters from 2009 still stands — he ran the last 20 meters celebrating in his world-record 200 the year before.
Nadia Comăneci scored the first perfect 10 in Olympic gymnastics history in 1976. The scoreboard, unable to display 10.0, showed 1.00.
Michael Phelps won 28 Olympic medals, 23 of them gold. As a country, he would rank in the top 40 all-time gold medal table.
Wilma Rudolph survived childhood polio that doctors said would keep her from walking — then won three sprint golds at the 1960 Olympics.
Jim Thorpe was stripped of his 1912 Olympic gold medals because he had been paid $25 a week to play minor league baseball — they were restored in 1983, 30 years after his death.
Olympic gold medals are mostly silver — they're required to contain only about 6 grams of actual gold.
The marathon is 26.2 miles because the 1908 London course was stretched so it could start at Windsor Castle and finish in front of the royal box.
Babe Didrikson Zaharias won Olympic gold in the hurdles and javelin, then took up golf and won 10 major championships. Many still call her the greatest all-around athlete ever.
Muhammad Ali, hands shaking from Parkinson's, lit the Olympic flame in Atlanta in 1996 — a moment kept secret until he stepped from the shadows.
Rocky Marciano retired 49-0 — still the only heavyweight champion to finish a career undefeated.
Boxing's first million-dollar gate was Dempsey-Carpentier in 1921, in a wooden stadium built just for the fight — and torn down right after.
The Indy 500 winner drinks milk in victory lane because 1936 winner Louis Meyer asked for buttermilk — a dairy executive saw the photo and made it tradition.
Danica Patrick's 2008 win in Japan remains the only IndyCar race ever won by a woman.
NASCAR's roots are in Prohibition: the best early drivers learned outrunning federal agents with trunks full of moonshine.
Richard Petty won 200 NASCAR races — exactly double second place — including 27 wins in a single season.
Secretariat's heart was found to weigh roughly 22 pounds — about two and a half times normal — a genetic quirk now called the 'x-factor.'
Bo Jackson is the only athlete named an All-Star in both MLB and the NFL Pro Bowl — and he considered football his hobby.
Basketball was invented in 1891 by James Naismith with a soccer ball and two peach baskets. The first game was 9-on-9 and ended 1-0.
Volleyball was invented in 1895 just 10 miles from basketball's birthplace — designed as a gentler alternative for older businessmen.
The America's Cup, first contested in 1851, is the oldest trophy in international sport — older than the modern Olympics by 45 years.
The 'hat trick' came to hockey from cricket, where a 19th-century bowler who took three wickets in a row was awarded a hat by his club.
